summary: At school, I was known for being strong in Sumo wrestling. I believe I was the third strongest in Sumo at my school, and a young teacher named Mr. Kobayashi from Osaka helped instruct me well in it. Sumo was a significant part of my youth and school life, even though I was a naughty boy. It remains an old dream and a memorable part of my childhood.

Mr. Kobayashi was a young teacher from Osaka who taught me at school. He instructed me well in Sumo, which was one of my strengths. His guidance left a lasting impression on me during my school days. I remember him as one of the good teachers who helped shape my early experiences.

Osaka is where Mr. Kobayashi, my Sumo instructor at school, came from. Though I did not live there, the connection through my teacher made me aware of Osaka. It was part of my early education experience in Japan before I came to America. Osaka represented a link to my schooling and training in Sumo.

I learned about Sonkoku Ninomiya in school, and his teachings influenced me. The story that impressed me most was Masashige Kusunoki's instruction to his son before the battle at Minatogawa, which I learned alongside Ninomiya's teachings. These lessons taught me about courage and rising up in difficult times. They stayed with me throughout my life and supported my morale.

Masashige Kusunoki's instruction to his son before the battle at Minatogawa left a strong impression on me. He told his son that a lion kicks its 3-day-old cub down a cliff to test its strength, encouraging him to be brave and rise up even in bad situations. This teaching about courage and perseverance was the most memorable lesson I learned in school. It helped me maintain morale during difficult times.

Minatogawa is the place where Masashige Kusunoki sent his son to battle, and I learned about this in school. The story of Kusunoki's teaching to his son before the battle there was very inspiring to me. It symbolized bravery and the importance of rising up in adversity. Minatogawa became a symbol of courage in the lessons I valued.

My family practiced the Zen sect of Buddhism. My grandparents were very religious people, and we observed many traditional customs at home, such as not cooking meat and offering cooked rice at the family altar before breakfast. Religion was an important part of our daily life and upbringing. The Zen faith influenced the values and discipline I grew up with.

I was very little during the Russo-Japanese War, so I don't remember much about it. Although I was too young to recall details, the war was a significant event in Japan's history around my early childhood. I mostly heard about it indirectly rather than from personal experience. It was a background event during my youth.

When I first came to America, our ship stopped at Vancouver. I was impressed by the beautiful scenery, with lawns along the beach and houses painted white and red. It was my first time seeing a foreign country, and Vancouver looked like a picture from the ship. That memory stayed with me as a vivid impression of my arrival in North America.

After Vancouver, we went to Seattle where I landed in America. I stayed at the Immigration Office there for two or three days before being allowed to enter. At Seattle, I had my eyes examined, but there was no full physical examination. It was an important step in my immigration process and my first real experience in the U.S.

After arriving in Seattle, I took a ship to San Pedro, Los Angeles, where my father greeted me for the first time. We stayed briefly in Japantown before going to San Pedro to start working on the farm. I remember thinking the hill there was a terrible place as we began clearing the land using horses. San Pedro was where I began my life working hard in America.

Japantown in Los Angeles was where we stayed for a night or two after I arrived in America. It was a community where many Japanese gathered, and I remember that in those days, the Japanese worked hard and had a good reputation. Japantown was also where the Tokyo Club, a gambling house, was located. It was an important cultural and social center for Japanese immigrants like me.

During harvest season, Japanese farmers often hired Mexican workers because there were not enough Japanese laborers. My father had one partner, and they used mostly Mexican workers to help with the farm work. This cooperation was common in those days, especially in places like San Pedro. The Mexican people played a significant role in supporting Japanese farming efforts.

I worked at orange ranches in Riverside, California, where Japanese bosses gathered 20 to 30 farm laborers. These camps were common in those days but no longer exist now. Working at the orange ranch was part of my early labor experience in America. Riverside was an important place for Japanese agricultural work during that period.

The Tokyo Club was a gambling house located in Japantown, San Pedro. Many single men, including Japanese, frequented it and led fast lives involving gambling. I remember that gambling was common among single men in those days. The Tokyo Club was a notable social spot within the Japanese community, though it represented a risky lifestyle.

During the Great Depression, I managed a small grocery store on Washington Blvd for about a year or two. Business was dull, and many customers paid by check, which was difficult to cash. The Depression was a hard time economically, and I had to adapt by running this store. It was a challenging period that affected my livelihood.

I experienced exclusion in Colorado after the war broke out when I was refused service at a restaurant. That was the only time I personally felt such discrimination. Before that, I had not been excluded individually because I belonged to a church where white people were more accepting. Colorado was where I first encountered that kind of racial exclusion.
The transcript does not mention any direct interaction or experience with the Federal Bureau of Investigation. Therefore, I have no personal story or relationship to share about the FBI.

The transcript does not provide specific details about my involvement or experiences with the Kenjin Kai organization. I cannot recall any particular memories related to Kenjin Kai from the interview.
There is no mention of the Japanese Training Fleet in my recollections or experiences shared in the transcript. I do not have a personal connection to this organization.
Lincoln Park is not mentioned in my oral history, so I have no memories or experiences related to that place to share.

The Immigration Act of 1924 had a significant impact on the Japanese community. Before 1924, many Japanese women came to America, but after the law took effect, immigration was severely restricted. I started going to church around 1924, and at that time, the Japanese community was still small but religious. The act shaped the demographics and social life of Japanese immigrants like me.
